starting_rubric.title: Bad Actors Modeling - SmartCity Case Study

criteria.name: Bad Actor Actions
criteria.description: 1 point per bad actor action (total = 3 points)
criteria.scoring_guidance:
Each of the 3 actions is worth 1 point, broken into two 0.5-point sub-criteria below.

Penalizations: Actions too similar: Two actions are too similar to each other Trigger: Two actions are too similar to each other Deduction: -0.5. Negative impact insufficiently described: Negative impact is not sufficiently described Trigger: Negative impact is not sufficiently described Deduction: -0.25.

sub_criteria.name: Action corresponds to category
sub_criteria.description: The action described corresponds to the category (check the cheatsheet)
sub_criteria.scoring_guidance: 0.5 points per action (×3 actions = 1.5 total). Check the cheatsheet to verify the action fits the chosen Bad Actors Motivation Category.

sub_criteria.name: Harmful impact specific to SmartCity
sub_criteria.description: The description makes clear how the action is harmful / negatively impacts stakeholders and is specific to SmartCity (i.e. adapted to the scenario)
sub_criteria.scoring_guidance: 0.5 points per action (×3 actions = 1.5 total). The description must clearly convey harm or negative impact on stakeholders and must be adapted to the SmartCity scenario.

metadata.parsing_notes: The rubric defines 1 point per bad actor action (3 actions = 3 points total), with each action scored on two 0.5-point dimensions. This is modeled as one 3-point parent criterion with two 1.5-point sub-criteria (each representing 0.5 × 3 actions). The first sub-criterion references an external "cheatsheet" for verifying that actions correspond to their chosen category; this dependency is preserved as-is and not resolved from the teaching material. The penalizations are listed separately as conditional deductions. It is somewhat ambiguous whether the -0.25 penalty for insufficient negative impact description applies per action or globally; the rubric does not specify, so it is preserved as written.

penalizations.name: Actions too similar
penalizations.description: Two actions are too similar to each other
penalizations.penalty_trigger: Two actions are too similar to each other

penalizations.name: Negative impact insufficiently described
penalizations.description: Negative impact is not sufficiently described
penalizations.penalty_trigger: Negative impact is not sufficiently described

improved_rubric.title: Bad Actors Modeling - SmartCity Case Study

criteria.name: Bad Actor Actions
criteria.description: 3 points total for three bad actor actions, scored across four dimensions: (1) whether each action's behaviour fits a named Bad Actors Motivation Category (1.0 pt), (2) whether the three actions are distinct from each other (0.5 pt), (3) whether each action describes a concrete harmful consequence (0.75 pt), and (4) whether each action and its harm are adapted to the SmartCity scenario (0.75 pt).
criteria.scoring_guidance:
Score each of the four sub-criteria independently. The student should provide exactly 3 bad actor actions.

Note: The former penalty 'Actions too similar (-0.5)' is now handled by the 'Actions are distinct from each other' sub-criterion (0.5 pt). The former penalty 'Negative impact insufficiently described (-0.25)' is now handled by the 'Harmful consequence is explicitly described' sub-criterion (0.75 pt). Do not apply these as separate deductions — they are built into the sub-criteria scoring.

sub_criteria.name: Action corresponds to category
sub_criteria.description: Each action's behaviour fits a recognised Bad Actors Motivation Category and the three actions are distinct. Scored via two sub-criteria below.
sub_criteria.scoring_guidance: 1.5 points total, scored via two sub-criteria: (1) Action behaviour fits a named motivation category (1.0 pt, scored 0.33 per action), and (2) Actions are distinct from each other (0.5 pt). See sub-criteria for detailed guidance. The five motivation categories are: Money (data theft, ransomware, competitor sabotage), Politics (data disclosure, disinformation, strategic sabotage, infrastructure attacks), Ideas (belief-spreading, discrediting groups, DoS, data leaks, defacement), Self-Interest (harassment, monitoring, tracking, grade changing), Entertainment (browsing private data, random system attacks, misinformation).

sub_criteria.name: Action behaviour fits a named motivation category
sub_criteria.description: Each action's described behaviour is recognisably consistent with at least one of the five Bad Actors Motivation Categories: Money (data theft, ransomware, sabotage by competitor), Politics (data disclosure, disinformation, strategic sabotage, infrastructure attacks), Ideas (belief-spreading, discrediting groups, DoS, data leaks, website defacement), Self-Interest (harassing messages, monitoring, tracking, grade changing), Entertainment (browsing private data, attacks on random systems, misinformation spreading). Award 0.33 points per action (×3 = 1.0) whose behaviour plausibly matches the category the student assigned it to. If the student did not name a category but the behaviour clearly fits one, still award credit. If the behaviour does not match any category, award 0.
sub_criteria.scoring_guidance: For each of the 3 actions, check: does the described malicious behaviour match the type of activity listed under the student's chosen motivation category (or any category if none is stated)? Categories overlap, so accept reasonable fits. Award 0.33 per action that fits. Total: 1.0 points.

sub_criteria.name: Actions are distinct from each other
sub_criteria.description: The three actions describe meaningfully different malicious behaviours (different attack vectors, different targets, or different methods). Two actions that describe essentially the same attack (e.g., both lock a dashboard for ransom) are not distinct even if assigned to different motivation categories.
sub_criteria.scoring_guidance: Award 0.5 if all three actions are clearly distinct. Award 0.25 if two actions are distinct but the third substantially overlaps with one of them. Award 0 if two or more pairs of actions are essentially the same behaviour. Judge by the concrete attack described, not just the category label.

sub_criteria.name: Harmful impact specific to SmartCity
sub_criteria.description: Each action describes a concrete harmful consequence and that consequence is specific to the SmartCity scenario. Scored via two sub-criteria below.
sub_criteria.scoring_guidance: 1.5 points total, scored via two sub-criteria: (1) Harmful consequence is explicitly described (0.75 pt, scored 0.25 per action), and (2) Action and harm are adapted to the SmartCity scenario (0.75 pt, scored 0.25 per action). See sub-criteria for detailed guidance.

sub_criteria.name: Harmful consequence is explicitly described
sub_criteria.description: For each action, the student describes a concrete negative consequence — i.e., what goes wrong and who is affected (stakeholders, citizens, city operations, etc.). Vague statements like 'this is negative for people' or 'stakeholders are not comfortable' do NOT count. The consequence must name or clearly imply a specific type of harm (e.g., data breach, service disruption, financial loss, safety risk, privacy violation, reputational damage).
sub_criteria.scoring_guidance: Award 0.25 per action (×3 = 0.75 total). For each action, check: does the student state a concrete harmful outcome? The outcome must go beyond a generic negative adjective — it should identify the type of harm (e.g., 'citizens' location data is exposed' or 'waste collection is disrupted'). Award 0.25 if yes, 0 if the harm is absent or only vaguely gestured at.

sub_criteria.name: Action and harm are adapted to the SmartCity scenario
sub_criteria.description: The action and its harmful consequence reference specific SmartCity infrastructure, services, or stakeholders — e.g., traffic sensors, waste-bin sensors, connected cameras, city dashboards, weather/heatwave alerts, 5G network, public app, city authorities, citizens using SmartCity services. A generic cyber-attack description (e.g., 'hackers steal data') that could apply to any system does NOT earn credit; the student must show how the attack exploits or disrupts SmartCity specifically.
sub_criteria.scoring_guidance: Award 0.25 per action (×3 = 0.75 total). For each action, check: does the description reference at least one concrete SmartCity component, service, or stakeholder group, AND does the described harm arise from the SmartCity context (not just a generic IT attack)? Award 0.25 if yes, 0 if the action/harm is generic or not tied to SmartCity.

metadata.parsing_notes: The rubric defines 1 point per bad actor action (3 actions = 3 points total), with each action scored on two 0.5-point dimensions. This is modeled as one 3-point parent criterion with two 1.5-point sub-criteria (each representing 0.5 × 3 actions). The first sub-criterion references an external "cheatsheet" for verifying that actions correspond to their chosen category; this dependency is preserved as-is and not resolved from the teaching material. The penalizations are listed separately as conditional deductions. It is somewhat ambiguous whether the -0.25 penalty for insufficient negative impact description applies per action or globally; the rubric does not specify, so it is preserved as written.

penalizations.description: Two actions are too similar to each other

findings.observation: Pairwise comparison found a winner for responses 8 and 9, but criterion a8a95866-fc85-4e83-ad6d-0a59f4e6204b>1b277fd0-98d7-42c7-8cc6-56788c953a4b gave near-equal scores.
findings.evidence: winner=B, confidence=0.92, a_score=0.67, b_score=0.75; Response B is stronger overall on the rubric. For action-category fit, B has three distinct actions that better match the motivation categories: ransom attack for Money, politically motivated traffic disruption for Politics, and anti-smart-tech belief-driven opposition for Ideas. In A, the Entertainment action repeats the dashboard-locking idea from Money, triggering the 'actions too similar' concern, and its harms are vaguer. For SmartCity-specific harmful impact, B gives clearer scenario-adapted consequences (service disruption, blocked emergency vehicles, trust damage), while A’s impacts are less specific and one action is explicitly uncertain. B’s Ideas example is weaker because it is more like online complaining than a concrete malicious action, but B still outperforms A across the criteria.

confidence.rationale: pairwise comparison against simulated criterion scores

findings.observation: Pairwise comparison found a winner for responses 0 and 2, but criterion a8a95866-fc85-4e83-ad6d-0a59f4e6204b>1b277fd0-98d7-42c7-8cc6-56788c953a4b gave near-equal scores.
findings.evidence: winner=B, confidence=0.89, a_score=0.83, b_score=0.89; Response B is stronger overall on both rubric subcriteria. Its three actions more clearly match motivation categories (money/competitor sabotage for market gain, politics/election-motivated disruption, ideas/privacy-motivated camera interference) and it gives more SmartCity-specific harms to stakeholders (traffic disruption, overflowing bins, traffic-light malfunction, reputational and financial damage). Response A has one clearly fitting action (politics/intelligence gathering) and one plausible entertainment action, but the money example focuses on stealing physical equipment rather than malicious use of the software/system, and several harms are generic or underdeveloped. B also provides more scenario-adapted impacts across all three actions.

confidence.rationale: pairwise comparison against simulated criterion scores

findings.observation: Pairwise comparison found a winner for responses 3 and 4, but criterion a8a95866-fc85-4e83-ad6d-0a59f4e6204b>1b277fd0-98d7-42c7-8cc6-56788c953a4b gave near-equal scores.
findings.evidence: winner=B, confidence=0.97, a_score=0.00, b_score=0.00; Response B is slightly stronger because it at least references relevant bad-actor motivation categories ('hackers', 'money', 'politics'), whereas Response A is entirely nonsensical and unrelated to the SmartCity bad-actor task. However, neither response provides three valid actions or SmartCity-specific harmful impacts, so both fail criterion a8a95866-fc85-4e83-ad6d-0a59f4e6204b and its subcriteria. The distinction is based on minimal topical relevance rather than substantive rubric fulfillment.

confidence.rationale: pairwise comparison against simulated criterion scores

findings.observation: Pairwise comparison found a winner for responses 3 and 4, but criterion a8a95866-fc85-4e83-ad6d-0a59f4e6204b>22f62c9d-13dd-4ac6-8d35-facb2c6a4910 gave near-equal scores.
findings.evidence: winner=B, confidence=0.97, a_score=0.00, b_score=0.00; Response B is slightly stronger because it at least references relevant bad-actor motivation categories ('hackers', 'money', 'politics'), whereas Response A is entirely nonsensical and unrelated to the SmartCity bad-actor task. However, neither response provides three valid actions or SmartCity-specific harmful impacts, so both fail criterion a8a95866-fc85-4e83-ad6d-0a59f4e6204b and its subcriteria. confidence.rationale: pairwise comparison against simulated criterion scores

findings.observation: Pairwise comparison found a winner for responses 4 and 5, but criterion a8a95866-fc85-4e83-ad6d-0a59f4e6204b>1b277fd0-98d7-42c7-8cc6-56788c953a4b gave near-equal scores.
findings.evidence: winner=B, confidence=0.95, a_score=0.00, b_score=0.03; Response B is clearly stronger. Response A does not provide three bad actor actions, does not map actions to motivation categories, and gives no SmartCity-specific harmful impacts, failing both subcriteria under the bad actor actions rubric. Response B is poorly written but at least references SmartCity elements (weather, traffic, waste-bins, dashboard, authorities, 5G, cameras, green lights) and implies some harmful impacts on stakeholders, so it better addresses the SmartCity-specific harm criterion, even though it still lacks three clear, well-categorized actions.

confidence.rationale: pairwise comparison against simulated criterion scores

findings.observation: Pairwise comparison found a winner for responses 0 and 8, but criterion a8a95866-fc85-4e83-ad6d-0a59f4e6204b>22f62c9d-13dd-4ac6-8d35-facb2c6a4910 gave near-equal scores.
findings.evidence: winner=A, confidence=0.84, a_score=0.87, b_score=0.79; Response A is stronger overall on the rubric. It provides three distinct actions that mostly fit their motivation categories and are adapted to the SmartCity scenario, with clearer stakeholder harm for each (foreign intelligence surveillance; theft of city infrastructure hardware; sensor manipulation disrupting waste collection). Response B has one solid Money action and one plausible Politics action, but its Entertainment action repeats the dashboard-locking idea from Money, triggering the 'actions too similar' concern, and its harms are described more vaguely. Thus A performs better on both category fit and SmartCity-specific harmful impact.

confidence.rationale: pairwise comparison against simulated criterion scores

proposed_changes.rationale: Update the parent criterion's description and scoring guidance to reflect the new sub-criteria structure. The original description says '1 point per bad actor action (total = 3 points)' with 'two 0.5-point sub-criteria', but after splitting each sub-criterion into finer dimensions, the structure is now four sub-criteria (1.0 + 0.5 + 0.75 + 0.75 = 3.0). The parent description must be updated to avoid contradicting the new layout.

confidence.rationale: LLM planner with grounding validation

proposed_changes.after: 3 points total for three bad actor actions, scored across four dimensions: (1) whether each action's behaviour fits a named Bad Actors Motivation Category (1.0 pt), (2) whether the three actions are distinct from each other (0.5 pt), (3) whether each action describes a concrete harmful consequence (0.75 pt), and (4) whether each action and its harm are adapted to the SmartCity scenario (0.75 pt).

proposed_changes.rationale: Update the 'Action corresponds to category' sub-criterion description to reflect that it is now a branch with two children, and to inline the category definitions from the teaching material instead of referencing an external cheatsheet.

confidence.rationale: LLM planner with grounding validation

The 'actions too similar' penalty is now absorbed into the 'Actions are distinct' sub-criterion. The 'negative impact insufficiently described' penalty is absorbed into the 'Harmful consequence is explicitly described' sub-criterion. This eliminates ambiguity about penalty scope.

proposed_changes.before: 0.5 points per action (×3 actions = 1.5 total). The description must clearly convey harm or negative impact on stakeholders and must be adapted to the SmartCity scenario.
proposed_changes.after: 1.5 points total, scored via two sub-criteria: (1) Harmful consequence is explicitly described (0.75 pt, scored 0.25 per action), and (2) Action and harm are adapted to the SmartCity scenario (0.75 pt, scored 0.25 per action). See sub-criteria for detailed guidance.

proposed_changes.rationale: Split 'Action corresponds to category' into two sub-criteria to improve discrimination. The first sub-criterion checks whether each action's behaviour plausibly fits one of the five named Bad Actors Motivation Categories from the teaching material (Money, Politics, Ideas, Self-Interest, Entertainment), with the category definitions inlined so graders don't need an external cheatsheet. This addresses both the applicability gap (graders unsure what 'corresponds to category' means) and the discrimination weakness (pairwise winners getting near-equal scores because the criterion is too coarse).

confidence.rationale: LLM planner with grounding validation

node.name: Action behaviour fits a named motivation category
node.description: Each action's described behaviour is recognisably consistent with at least one of the five Bad Actors Motivation Categories: Money (data theft, ransomware, sabotage by competitor), Politics (data disclosure, disinformation, strategic sabotage, infrastructure attacks), Ideas (belief-spreading, discrediting groups, DoS, data leaks, website defacement), Self-Interest (harassing messages, monitoring, tracking, grade changing), Entertainment (browsing private data, attacks on random systems, misinformation spreading). Award 0.33 points per action (×3 = 1.0) whose behaviour plausibly matches the category the student assigned it to. If the student did not name a category but the behaviour clearly fits one, still award credit. If the behaviour does not match any category, award 0.
node.scoring_guidance: For each of the 3 actions, check: does the described malicious behaviour match the type of activity listed under the student's chosen motivation category (or any category if none is stated)? Categories overlap, so accept reasonable fits. Award 0.33 per action that fits. Total: 1.0 points.

proposed_changes.rationale: The second sub-criterion under 'Action corresponds to category' checks whether the three actions are sufficiently distinct from each other. This absorbs the existing penalty for 'actions too similar' into a positive scoring dimension, making it clearer and improving discrimination — the pairwise evidence shows that responses with overlapping actions (e.g., repeating dashboard-locking across Money and Entertainment) were not reliably penalised.

confidence.rationale: LLM planner with grounding validation

node.name: Actions are distinct from each other
node.description: The three actions describe meaningfully different malicious behaviours (different attack vectors, different targets, or different methods). Two actions that describe essentially the same attack (e.g., both lock a dashboard for ransom) are not distinct even if assigned to different motivation categories.
node.scoring_guidance: Award 0.5 if all three actions are clearly distinct. Award 0.25 if two actions are distinct but the third substantially overlaps with one of them. Award 0 if two or more pairs of actions are essentially the same behaviour. Judge by the concrete attack described, not just the category label.

proposed_changes.rationale: Split 'Harmful impact specific to SmartCity' into two sub-criteria to resolve the high ambiguity (α=0.65). Graders disagree because the current criterion bundles two distinct dimensions: (1) whether the student describes a concrete negative consequence at all, and (2) whether that consequence is tied to SmartCity infrastructure. This first sub-criterion isolates the 'harm is explicitly described' dimension. The evidence shows graders diverge most when a response mentions vague phrases like 'negative for people' — this sub-criterion clarifies what counts as a described harmful impact.

confidence.rationale: LLM planner with grounding validation

node.name: Harmful consequence is explicitly described
node.description: For each action, the student describes a concrete negative consequence — i.e., what goes wrong and who is affected (stakeholders, citizens, city operations, etc.). Vague statements like 'this is negative for people' or 'stakeholders are not comfortable' do NOT count. The consequence must name or clearly imply a specific type of harm (e.g., data breach, service disruption, financial loss, safety risk, privacy violation, reputational damage).
node.scoring_guidance: Award 0.25 per action (×3 = 0.75 total). For each action, check: does the student state a concrete harmful outcome? The outcome must go beyond a generic negative adjective — it should identify the type of harm (e.g., 'citizens' location data is exposed' or 'waste collection is disrupted'). Award 0.25 if yes, 0 if the harm is absent or only vaguely gestured at.

proposed_changes.rationale: This second sub-criterion under 'Harmful impact specific to SmartCity' isolates the SmartCity-specificity dimension. The evidence shows graders disagree on whether mentioning SmartCity components (cameras, sensors, dashboards, traffic lights, bins) is sufficient or whether the harm itself must be scenario-specific. This sub-criterion clarifies: the action and its harm must reference concrete SmartCity infrastructure or services, not just generic cyber-attack consequences.

confidence.rationale: LLM planner with grounding validation

node.name: Action and harm are adapted to the SmartCity scenario
node.description: The action and its harmful consequence reference specific SmartCity infrastructure, services, or stakeholders — e.g., traffic sensors, waste-bin sensors, connected cameras, city dashboards, weather/heatwave alerts, 5G network, public app, city authorities, citizens using SmartCity services. A generic cyber-attack description (e.g., 'hackers steal data') that could apply to any system does NOT earn credit; the student must show how the attack exploits or disrupts SmartCity specifically.
node.scoring_guidance: Award 0.25 per action (×3 = 0.75 total). For each action, check: does the description reference at least one concrete SmartCity component, service, or stakeholder group, AND does the described harm arise from the SmartCity context (not just a generic IT attack)? Award 0.25 if yes, 0 if the action/harm is generic or not tied to SmartCity.
